name: Great Zimbabwe
description: Great Zimbabwe is an ancient city in the south-eastern hills of Zimbabwe. Constructed from the 11th to the 15th centuries, it was a thriving centre of trade and governance for the ancestors of the Shona people. While mainstream archaeology attributes its construction and prominence firmly to indigenous African populations, some alternative theories propose origins linked to outside civilizations, such as Phoenicians, Egyptians, or even the biblical Queen of Sheba. Proponents of these alternative theories often cite the impressive scale and sophistication of the architecture, particularly the dry stone walls, as evidence beyond the capabilities of the local population at the time. They may also point to perceived similarities between Great Zimbabwe's structures and those found in other ancient civilizations. Mainstream scholarship strongly refutes these claims, providing substantial archaeological, historical, and anthropological evidence demonstrating indigenous African origin and development. This includes the presence of distinctly African pottery, tools, and burial practices, as well as oral traditions and written accounts from early European explorers that document the presence of a thriving Shona civilization. The alternative theories are often criticized as reflecting a colonial-era bias that denies or minimizes the achievements of African societies.
subtitle: Ancient city and alternative origin theories.

claims.claim: Great Zimbabwe was built by a non-African civilization.
claims.evidence: The sophistication of the architecture is deemed too advanced for the local population at the time.
claims.criticism: Archaeological evidence overwhelmingly supports indigenous African construction and development, including locally produced artifacts and established building techniques.

claims.claim: Great Zimbabwe is the legendary Ophir of the Bible, the source of King Solomon's gold.
claims.evidence: Some historical accounts and interpretations of biblical texts suggest a connection between Zimbabwe and gold mines.
claims.criticism: There is no definitive archaeological evidence linking Great Zimbabwe to Ophir. The location of Ophir remains debated among biblical scholars, and the association with Zimbabwe is based on speculation rather than concrete proof.

claims.claim: The layout and design of Great Zimbabwe possess hidden astronomical or esoteric significance.
claims.evidence: Some researchers have proposed alignments with celestial bodies or geometric patterns with symbolic meaning.
claims.criticism: Such interpretations are often based on selective data and lack rigorous scientific validation. The practical functions and societal organization of the city offer a more plausible explanation for its design.

keyFigures.name: David Randall-MacIver
keyFigures.role: Early archaeologist who correctly attributed the site to African origins.

keyFigures.name: Richard Hall
keyFigures.role: Early archaeologist who promoted the idea of non-African origins.

timeline.event: Great Zimbabwe reaches its peak as a center of trade and governance.
Decline of Great Zimbabwe, possibly due to environmental factors or political changes.
European exploration and archaeological investigation begins, leading to conflicting interpretations of its origins.

sources.author: Huffman, Thomas N.
sources.work: Snakes & Crocodiles: Power and Symbolism in Ancient Zimbabwe
sources.title: Huffman, Thomas N.
